title: Regime-specific post-hoc recalibration tree on out-of-fold scores
summary: Public sources on calibration show that one global map can miss region-specific distortions, and beta calibration is often safer than plain logistic scaling for skewed score distributions.
rationale: Public sources on calibration show that one global map can miss region-specific distortions, and beta calibration is often safer than plain logistic scaling for skewed score distributions. For ROC AUC, a global monotone map changes little, but piecewise or regime-specific calibration can reorder examples across wet/dry, early/late, or debt/no-debt states.
implementation_hint: Generate out-of-fold base scores, then fit a very small calibration tree or segmented beta calibrators using only coarse state variables such as wet vs slick, Monaco-like exception races, early/mid/late race, fresh vs old tyre, and rule-debt status. Blend calibrated scores only if race-held-out CV improves, since this is a cheap second-stage experiment.
expected_effect: Likely a modest but low-cost lift that mainly improves generalization from local CV to public leaderboard by correcting regime-dependent score distortion.
risk: Too many segments will overfit and hurt AUC. Keep the partition shallow, train only on OOF predictions, and evaluate on strict grouped folds.
